explanation: This LTL property states that, at least once in every time unit (i.e., every second), a matrix update must be sent from DM1 to DM2, DM3, DM4, and DM5. This ensures that every DM receives the latest matrix information once per second, thereby enabling the local instance update of matrix M with frequency 1 as specified. The property matches the requirement that all DMs are updated within 1 time unit.

The natural language statement requires each DM (Distributed Module) to transmit its local instance of matrix M to other DMs within 1 time unit, ensuring that updates occur every second so all DUs can keep their navigation data current. To capture this behavior, we select an LTL property that states: for every 1 time unit (here, N_DURATION1 is set to 1 to represent 1 second), it is always the case that either DM1 sends the update to DM2, DM3, DM4, or DM5. This ensures all other DMs get the updated matrix from DM1 within each time frame, which can be extrapolated to any DM if expanded accordingly. This captures the periodicity and timely delivery requirements stated in the input.

The requirement specifies that every DU's instance of matrix M must be updated every second, enabling all DUs to update their navigation data each second, and each DM must send its local matrix instance to other DMs within 1 time unit. This is essentially a liveness guarantee with a timing constraint on data distribution: every second, each DM communicates its updated matrix to the others. We model this as: every time a clock event occurs (representing the 1-second tick), each DM must send its local matrix to every other DM within 1 time unit. There is an atomic proposition for the clock tick (clock_occurred) and propositions for each DM1 sending a matrix update to DM2, DM3, DM4, and DM5. So, upon clock_occurred, within 1 unit of time, all of these matrix updates must be sent. This is best expressed as: whenever clock_occurred happens, then in the next 1 time unit, all the matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dmX must occur. In LTL with timing, this equates to: Globally, if clock_occurred, then within [0,1] time units, the conjunction of all required matrix update sends must hold (i.e., must be true at some point in that interval). Therefore, we use template 94: G (bool_exp1 -> ( (bool_exp3) U[0,N_DURATION1] (bool_exp4))), where bool_exp1 is clock_occurred, bool_exp3 can be TRUE or omitted (to permit immediate satisfaction), and bool_exp4 is the conjunction of all required matrix update sends. Set N_DURATION1 to 1.

The requirement states that each DU must update its instance of matrix M every second so that navigation data is refreshed every second, and each DM must transmit its local matrix instance to other DMs within 1 time unit (i.e., within 1 second). There are atomic propositions for each specific matrix update being sent from DM1 to each of DM2, DM3, DM4, DM5, as well as each being received. We need to guarantee that in every 1 time unit interval, all other DMs (DM2 through DM5) receive a matrix update from DM1. The best way to formalize 'each DM must receive a matrix update from DM1 at least once every second' is to use a global 'within every 1 unit interval, the matrix update is received'—for each of the DMs 2/3/4/5. Since the template requires a single boolean expression, we can express this as the conjunction of the reception of matrix updates by DM1 from DM2, DM3, DM4, and DM5. Thus, the LTL formula will be: G[0,1] (matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm2 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm3 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm4 & matrix_update_received_dm1_from_dm5). This means that in every sliding window of 1 time unit, DM1 must have received a matrix update from each of the other DMs, which is consistent with the requirement for every DU to receive updates from the others within 1 second. The same could be specified for each DMi, but with the names available, we specifically have only DM1 as receiver. This matches as closely as possible to the requirement with the given atomic props.

The natural language describes the requirement that every DM (Distributed Module) must send updates of its local matrix to every other DM every second, ensuring all DMs can update their navigation data every second (with a frequency of 1 Hz). This periodic exchange can be modeled as: 'every time a clock occurs (every time unit), for each DM, it sends matrix updates to each other DM.' Since the propositions are for concrete DMs (DM1-DM5), we capture that after every occurrence of the clock (''a clock has occurred''), all matrix update send actions (''matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2'', ..., ''matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5'') eventually happen within one time unit. Template 70 ('G (F[0,N_DURATION1] bool_exp3)') expresses the property 'globally, within every time window of N_DURATION1, bool_exp3 eventually becomes true.' Here, bool_exp3 is the join (AND) that every required matrix update is sent. N_DURATION1 is set to 1 (one time unit). This ensures all required matrix updates happen at least every second.

The requirement says that every DU (Distributed Unit) should update its navigation data every second. This means that within every 1 time unit (here, interpreted as 1 second), each Data Manager (DM) must transmit its local matrix instance to the other DMs. Thus, for every DM and every second, we must have all possible matrix updates between DMs occur. Since the atomic propositions refer explicitly to matrix update transmission between DM1 and DM2/3/4/5, we can enforce that all of these transmissions occur in every 1 time unit using the 'globally eventually' property. Thus, 'Globally, in every 1 time unit, all matrix update messages between DM1 and DM2, DM1 and DM3, DM1 and DM4, DM1 and DM5 are sent.' This is best captured by a template like G(F[0,1] ...), here formulated as G(F[0,1] (...)). We use conjunction to require all transmissions within the period.

The natural language states that each DM (Distributed Module) must transmit its local matrix to the other DMs within 1 time unit, and the local instance M for DU_i must be updated every 1 time unit for all DUs. Using the provided atomic propositions, the closest mapping is to express that, for example, within every 1 time unit, DM1 must send a matrix update to DM2, DM3, DM4, and DM5. Using basically
G[0,1] matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2 and similar, or equivalently, at least ensure within every time interval of length 1, these transmissions happen.

The strictest generalization is to require that globally, in every time window of size 1, these updates (matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2, and similar for DM3, DM4, DM5) happen. This is best captured by using template 21: G[0,N_DURATION1] bool_exp3. For the case here, bool_exp3 will be the conjunction of all the needed update sends.

Thus, the translation is: Within every 1 time unit (N_DURATION1=1), DM1 must send its matrix update to DM2, DM3, DM4, and DM5.

Note: The translation takes one DM for illustration due to given APs, but can be extended to other DMs similarly.

This property expresses that whenever a clock tick occurs ('clock_occurred'), it will always be the case, for the next 1 time unit, that at least one of the matrix update transmissions (from DM1 to DM2, DM3, DM4, or DM5) occurs. This reflects the requirement that each DM must update and transmit its local matrix instance every second, enabling all DUs to update navigation data every second, matching the 'frequency 1' constraint. The template ensures these update transmissions occur globally every second after each clock event, within the stated time bound. Since bool_exp4 is not required by the template, it is left empty, which is appropriate as per the template's structure (ID 154).

The provided property requires that every DM must send its local matrix update to another DM within 1 time unit, ensuring that navigation data is up to date every second. This is captured using the atomic proposition dm_i_sends_sync_to_dm_j, which represents a generic matrix update sending event between DMs. The formula globally requires that at every point, it is always the case that within the next 1 time unit, dm_i_sends_sync_to_dm_j occurs, which ensures the required frequency and timely updates for all DMs.

The natural language requirement implies that every DM must send its local matrix update to the other DMs at least every 1 time unit (i.e., once per second), so all DUs get updated navigation data each second. There are atomic propositions for sending updates from DM1 to DM2, DM3, DM4, and DM5, which can collectively represent the update activity. The strongest LTL guarantee here is that (globally) at every point in time, within every 1-unit window, at least one update to every peer occurs. This directly corresponds to G(F[0,1] P), where P is the conjunction of update-sending signals from DM1 to the others. Setting N_DURATION1=1, the template G(F[0,N_DURATION1] bool_exp3) is used, with bool_exp3 = (matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4 & matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5).
